title: Isotropic random walks and Brownian diffusion on complex projective space
abstract: We show that isotropic random walks on the complex projective space provide a canonical and analytically tractable stochastic-geometric framework for the exploration of quantum-state space. The approach combines harmonic analysis on compact rank-one symmetric spaces with stochastic pure-state evolution and yields explicit analytical expressions for transition kernels, fidelity statistics, and geometric observables associated with the Fubini--Study metric. In particular, the framework provides a solvable reference model for isotropic depolarization and Haar equilibration, reproducing Haar-random fidelity statistics and the invariant measure on projective Hilbert space without specifying a microscopic Lindblad generator. In the short-time regime, the stochastic evolution converges to Brownian diffusion generated by the Fubini--Study Laplace--Beltrami operator, while the long-time limit exhibits concentration-of-measure behaviour characteristic of high-dimensional random quantum states. We further derive analytical and asymptotic results for the first-passage-time problem, including closed-form expressions in the Brownian limit for the mean first passage time and the long-time tail of the first-passage-time distribution. For high-fidelity target states, the mean first passage time exhibits a strong dimension-dependent divergence originating from the concentration properties of the Fubini--Study geometry.
summary: The paper introduces an isotropic random walk model on the complex projective space to study quantum state evolution geometrically. By analyzing this process, it derives explicit formulas for fidelity decay and calculates the mean time required to reach a target state. This framework offers a powerful, solvable reference model for understanding decoherence dynamics in high-dimensional quantum systems.
main_problem: To develop an analytically tractable stochastic-geometric framework for understanding quantum state space exploration, particularly in the context of decoherence.
main_result: The isotropic random walk on complex projective space provides a solvable model that reproduces Haar-random fidelity statistics and yields analytical expressions for first-passage times. The mean time to reach high-fidelity states diverges strongly with dimension due to geometric concentration effects.
method: The analysis combines harmonic analysis on compact rank-one symmetric spaces with the study of random walks, which are shown to converge to Brownian diffusion governed by the Fubini–Study Laplace–Beltrami operator in the short/long time limits.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as an isotropic random walk on the complex projective space ($\mathbb{CP}^{n-1}$), which represents the pure quantum state space. This approach serves as a geometric reference model for open quantum evolution, bypassing the need to specify a microscopic Lindblad generator.
key_observables: Transition kernels, fidelity statistics ($F$), mean first passage time ($m^*$), and expectation values of geometric observables related to the Fubini–Study metric.
important_parameters: The dimension $n$ (related to the Hilbert space dimension), the number of steps $m$, and the target fidelity $F^*$ are crucial parameters governing convergence rates and divergence behavior.
main_assumptions: The model assumes an isotropic, Markovian evolution on $\mathbb{CP}^{n-1}$ and relies on approximating the random walk by Brownian diffusion in certain limits to solve for first passage times.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the general framework using harmonic analysis on symmetric spaces, derives transition probabilities via Jacobi polynomials, analyzes the short-time convergence to heat kernel/Brownian motion, and finally solves the mean first passage time problem by reducing it to a boundary value problem involving the killed Laplacian.
why_interesting: This work provides powerful geometric tools—solvable stochastic processes on curved manifolds—to analyze fundamental concepts in quantum information like scrambling and decoherence without needing specific Hamiltonian details. The dimension-dependent divergence of $m^*$ is particularly insightful for understanding state preparation difficulty.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
